questions.question: Article 40.5 of the Constitution provides that the dwelling of every citizen is inviolable and shall not be forcibly entered save 'in accordance with law'. Which of the following best states the judicial interpretation of 'in accordance with law' in this context?

options.A: Any statutory authority to enter a dwelling automatically satisfies Art. 40.5
options.B: Entry must be authorised by a valid statute, executed in accordance with proper procedures, and that statute must itself be constitutionally valid
options.C: Entry requires a warrant issued by a judge in all circumstances
options.D: The phrase means 'any established practice of law enforcement' including Garda custom and practice

questions.explanation: The 'in accordance with law' standard in Art. 40.5 requires more than a bare statutory authorisation: the authorising statute must be constitutionally valid, the power must be exercised in compliance with the statutory procedure, and the overall scheme must respect the constitutional principle of the dwelling's inviolability. See Ryan v AG [1965] IR 294 and subsequent Garda search power cases. Invalid statutory authority or non-compliance with statutory procedure renders entry unlawful.

questions.question: In DPP v Owens [1999] 2 IR 16, the Supreme Court considered the constitutional validity of a Garda search without a warrant under s.23 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977. The court upheld the search provision. On what basis?

options.A: The Supreme Court held that drug trafficking creates an emergency exception to the warrant requirement
options.B: The Supreme Court held that s.23 searches, while warrantless, were constitutionally permissible because they were authorised by statute, were limited to specific circumstances (reasonable grounds to believe drugs are present), and proportionate to the objective of combating serious drug crime
options.C: The Supreme Court held that Art. 40.5 does not apply to commercial premises used for drug distribution
options.D: The Supreme Court held that Art. 40.5 only protects the physical structure, not the privacy of activities within

questions.explanation: DPP v Owens [1999] illustrates that warrantless searches of dwellings may be constitutionally permissible if the statutory power is sufficiently circumscribed: the Garda must have reasonable grounds, the purpose must be specified (drug search), and the power must be proportionate. Art. 40.5 permits entry 'in accordance with law' — this does not require a judicial warrant in every case, but the statutory authorisation must have adequate safeguards to protect against arbitrary invasion.

questions.question: What is the constitutional significance of the word 'dwelling' in Article 40.5, and does the protection extend to commercial premises?

options.A: The protection extends to all premises owned by a citizen, including shops and offices
options.B: The protection is limited to the place used as a habitual residence — typically a private home; commercial premises do not enjoy Art. 40.5 protection
options.C: The protection extends to any place where a person has a reasonable expectation of privacy
options.D: The protection extends to any premises registered in a citizen’s name regardless of its use

questions.explanation: 'Dwelling' in Art. 40.5 refers to the place where a person lives — their home. Irish courts have interpreted this as protecting primarily residential premises: the place used as a home or habitation. Commercial premises, shops, offices and warehouses do not enjoy the constitutional protection of Art. 40.5 as a dwelling, although other constitutional provisions (right to property under Art. 43, general procedural rights) may still be engaged by arbitrary State intrusion. The constitutional protection is specifically for the sanctity of the home.

questions.question: In People (DPP) v McGrath [1990] 2 IR 38, the Supreme Court held that entry by Gardaí into a dwelling without valid authority was a breach of Art. 40.5. What was the consequence for evidence obtained during the unlawful entry?

options.A: Evidence obtained during an unconstitutional search is always admissible provided it is relevant and cogent
options.B: Evidence obtained in deliberate or conscious violation of constitutional rights must be excluded, unless exceptional circumstances justify admission (the DPP v Kenny [1990] exclusionary rule)
options.C: Evidence obtained during an unconstitutional search must be referred to the Director of Public Prosecutions for an admissibility assessment
options.D: Evidence obtained during an unlawful entry is admissible but the arresting officers may face civil liability

questions.explanation: Evidence obtained in deliberate or conscious violation of Art. 40.5 (and other constitutional rights) must generally be excluded under the constitutional exclusionary rule established in DPP v Kenny [1990] 2 IR 110. Where the Gardaí deliberately or consciously violated the constitutional right (e.g., entering without a valid warrant knowing one was required), the evidence obtained is excluded. Only in truly exceptional circumstances may evidence obtained in such violation be admitted.

questions.question: DPP v Kenny [1990] 2 IR 110 established the strict Irish exclusionary rule for evidence obtained in breach of constitutional rights. In People (DPP) v JC [2017] 1 IR 417, the Supreme Court revisited this rule. What did the court decide?

options.A: The court abolished the exclusionary rule entirely, adopting a pure balancing test
options.B: The court maintained the absolute exclusionary rule for all constitutional rights violations without modification
options.C: The court modified the Kenny rule: evidence is now presumptively excluded for deliberate violations, but a court has a discretion to admit evidence obtained in good faith in circumstances where the breach was not deliberate and resulted from an objectively reasonable but mistaken belief that the search was lawful
options.D: The court held that the exclusionary rule applies only to evidence obtained in breach of Art. 40.5, not other constitutional rights

questions.explanation: People (DPP) v JC [2017] 1 IR 417 (Clarke J, majority) modified the absolute DPP v Kenny [1990] exclusionary rule. The new JC test distinguishes between: (i) deliberate or conscious violations — evidence is excluded (maintaining Kenny); and (ii) inadvertent violations resulting from a good faith, objectively reasonable mistake — a discretion arises to admit evidence if the trial judge is satisfied the breach was not deliberate and admission would not bring the administration of justice into disrepute. This represents a significant softening of the strict Kenny approach.

questions.question: A Garda enters a dwelling under a search warrant issued by a Peace Commissioner (not a judge). The warrant is later found to have been issued without proper statutory authority. Which case directly addressed the constitutional validity of search warrants issued by Peace Commissioners?

options.A: DPP v Owens [1999] 2 IR 16 — held Peace Commissioner warrants always constitutionally valid
options.B: DPP v Delaney [1997] 3 IR 453 — questioned the constitutional validity of Peace Commissioner warrants, since a peace commissioner is not an independent judicial officer
options.C: People v Cunningham [1974] IR 19 — held that any person authorised by statute to issue warrants satisfies Art. 40.5
options.D: Ryan v AG [1965] IR 294 — established that judicial warrants are required for all dwelling searches

questions.explanation: DPP v Delaney [1997] 3 IR 453 raised concerns about Peace Commissioner-issued search warrants, as Peace Commissioners are not independent judicial officers and may not provide a sufficient constitutional safeguard for the Art. 40.5 right. Later cases (including DPP v Dunne [1994] and the Criminal Justice (Search Warrants) Act 2012) addressed this concern by requiring District Court judges rather than Peace Commissioners to issue warrants for serious offences. The constitutional concern is whether a non-judicial officer provides adequate protection for the right of inviolability.

questions.question: Under Article 40.5, can a statutory provision authorise Garda entry into a dwelling without prior judicial authorisation in cases of emergency?

options.A: No — Art. 40.5 absolutely requires a prior judicial warrant for all entries
options.B: Yes — Art. 40.5 permits entry 'in accordance with law'; where statute provides a narrow emergency power with sufficient safeguards, warrantless entry may be constitutionally permissible
options.C: Yes, but only during a declared national emergency under Art. 28.3.3°
options.D: No — any warrantless entry is automatically a violation of the Convention rights incorporated by the ECHR Act 2003

questions.explanation: Art. 40.5 does not require a judicial warrant in every case — it requires entry to be 'in accordance with law'. Statutes may validly authorise warrantless entry in genuine emergencies (e.g., to prevent serious harm, or where evidence would be lost before a warrant could be obtained) provided the statutory power is sufficiently constrained by conditions (reasonable grounds, specific purpose, urgency) and proportionate. The key is that the statutory framework must provide adequate protection against arbitrary or unreasonable intrusion.

questions.question: The Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009 permits Gardaí in limited circumstances to conduct covert surveillance of a dwelling. Which constitutional provision is primarily engaged, and how does the legislation address the constitutional concern?

options.A: Art. 40.5 is engaged; the Act addresses it by requiring prior ministerial approval and excluding all surveillance evidence from criminal proceedings
options.B: Art. 40.5 (and the unenumerated right to privacy) are engaged; the Act addresses constitutional concerns by requiring prior judicial authorisation from a Circuit Court judge and limiting the power to serious offences
options.C: Art. 40.6 (freedom of expression) is the primary provision; the Act limits surveillance to communications only
options.D: Art. 28.3.3° emergency power is the constitutional basis; civilian surveillance requires a declared emergency

questions.explanation: The Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009 engages both Art. 40.5 (inviolability of the dwelling) and the unenumerated right to privacy under Art. 40.3.1°. The Act addresses constitutional concerns through: (i) prior judicial authorisation by a Circuit Court judge; (ii) limitation to investigations of serious offences; (iii) strict conditions on duration and renewal. These safeguards are designed to ensure the surveillance power is 'in accordance with law' within the constitutional meaning.

questions.question: A landlord claims a right to enter a tenant's dwelling without notice for routine inspection. The tenant asserts Art. 40.5 protection. What is the most legally accurate analysis?

options.A: Art. 40.5 protects only against State intrusion — private persons such as landlords are not subject to the constitutional guarantee
options.B: Art. 40.5 applies horizontally to all persons, including landlords; any entry without consent is constitutionally prohibited
options.C: The tenant may assert Art. 40.5 as a constitutional norm that could inform the interpretation of landlord and tenant legislation and common law rights; an unilateral entry by a landlord without notice may breach the tenant's constitutional right to the inviolability of their home
options.D: Landlord inspection rights are exclusively a matter of the Residential Tenancies Act 2004 and are beyond constitutional adjudication

questions.explanation: Art. 40.5 is primarily directed at State intrusion but can also inform the legal framework governing private relations. The Residential Tenancies Act 2004 regulates landlord access (requiring reasonable notice), reflecting the constitutional value of dwelling inviolability. Irish courts may use Art. 40.5 as a principle of constitutional interpretation informing landlord-tenant rights, even where the direct horizontal application of the constitutional provision in private law is not fully established. An unlawful entry by a landlord would at minimum give rise to claims in tort (trespass).

questions.question: Which of the following most accurately distinguishes Art. 40.5 (inviolability of the dwelling) from the unenumerated right to privacy under Art. 40.3.1°?

options.A: Art. 40.5 is limited to physical intrusion into the dwelling; Art. 40.3 unenumerated privacy extends to any context in which a person has a reasonable expectation of privacy, including communications, personal data and private activities outside the home
options.B: There is no meaningful distinction — both provisions provide identical protection
options.C: Art. 40.5 applies to citizens only; Art. 40.3 unenumerated privacy applies to all persons present in Ireland
options.D: Art. 40.3 is limited to the right to bodily integrity; Art. 40.5 is the only provision that protects privacy

questions.explanation: Art. 40.5 is the specific constitutional guarantee for the physical inviolability of the home — it is a focused provision protecting against forced entry. The unenumerated right to privacy under Art. 40.3.1° is broader: it encompasses reasonable expectations of privacy in communications (Kennedy v Ireland [1987] IR 587), personal data, intimate relationships, and contexts beyond the physical home. Both provisions may overlap but Art. 40.3 is more flexible and applicable to novel privacy claims not directly involving physical dwelling intrusion.

questions.question: In Kennedy and Arnold v Ireland [1987] IR 587, Hamilton P recognised an unenumerated constitutional right to privacy. The plaintiffs were journalists whose phones were tapped. This case is notable because:

options.A: It held that phone tapping is absolutely prohibited by Art. 40.5
options.B: It recognised a constitutional right to privacy extending beyond the physical home to protect communications, and held that the State's tapping of journalists' phones without lawful authority was unconstitutional
options.C: It established that freedom of the press under Art. 40.6.1°(i) protects all journalistic sources from any form of surveillance
options.D: It held that privacy rights apply only to private residences and do not extend to professional communications

questions.explanation: Kennedy and Arnold v Ireland [1987] IR 587 (Hamilton P) recognised the unenumerated constitutional right to privacy under Art. 40.3.1° and held that this right extends to the privacy of telephone communications. The State's unauthorised tapping of the journalists' phones was held to be an unconstitutional invasion of privacy. This case is foundational for the broader right to privacy in Irish constitutional law, extending the protection beyond the dwelling to the privacy of communications generally.

questions.question: A planning inspector seeks to enter a dwelling to assess planning enforcement. The householder refuses entry. Under Art. 40.5, what must the inspector have to lawfully enter the dwelling against the owner's wishes?

options.A: A verbal instruction from the senior planning officer in the local authority
options.B: A written direction from the An Bord Pleanála chair
options.C: A warrant issued under the relevant planning legislation by a District Court judge or other authorised judicial officer, provided the statutory procedure is followed
options.D: No warrant is required — planning inspectors have an inherent statutory power to enter all premises under the Planning and Development Act 2000

questions.explanation: Under Art. 40.5, forced entry into a dwelling against the wishes of the occupant requires legal authorisation 'in accordance with law'. For planning enforcement, the Planning and Development Act 2000 provides that an authorised person may seek a warrant from the District Court to enter a dwelling where entry has been refused. Without such a warrant (or other statutory basis providing adequate safeguards), forced entry into a dwelling for planning enforcement purposes would violate Art. 40.5.

questions.question: Does Article 40.5 protect a person from having their home demolished by court order where the structure has been built without planning permission?

options.A: Yes — Art. 40.5 creates an absolute right preventing any court-ordered demolition of a dwelling
options.B: No — a court enforcement order under the Planning and Development Act 2000 made in accordance with due process satisfies the 'in accordance with law' standard; Art. 40.5 does not create an absolute right
options.C: Yes — an owner's right to their home always prevails over planning enforcement
options.D: No — planning enforcement demolition orders are immune from constitutional challenge by virtue of Art. 15.4

questions.explanation: Art. 40.5 does not create an absolute right to occupy a dwelling regardless of planning violations. A court enforcement order requiring demolition of an unauthorised dwelling, made in accordance with the Planning and Development Act 2000 following due process, constitutes a lawful interference with the dwelling 'in accordance with law'. The proportionality of such an order (balancing personal rights against community planning interests) is subject to judicial review, but Art. 40.5 does not automatically bar planning enforcement affecting a dwelling.
